title: Your First CLI Tool
description: Node.js + Claude API in one script. Build something you'll actually use.

learningObjectives.text: Set up a Node.js script that calls the Claude API
Build a CLI tool that takes input and produces output
Write a system prompt that makes your tool reliable

contentSections.title: CLI tools: the fastest path to useful

contentSections.body: A CLI tool has no UI to design, no hosting to configure, no auth to implement. It's just: run a command, get an answer. That's why every developer's most-used tool is one they wrote themselves in an afternoon.

### The anatomy of a CLI tool

Three pieces:
1. **Input** — command-line arguments
2. **Claude call** — system prompt defines the tool's behavior
3. **Output** — print the result

Everything else is optional.

### Setup
```bash
mkdir my-tool && cd my-tool
npm init -y
npm install @anthropic-ai/sdk
# Add to package.json: "type": "module"
```

contentSections.question: What's the minimum lines of code needed to call Claude and print the result?
contentSections.answer: **Fewer than 15 lines.** Import the SDK, create a client, call `messages.create()`, print `response.content[0].text`. The hard part isn't the code — it's the system prompt.
contentSections.hint: Think about what the Anthropic SDK handles for you.

contentSections.body: The code is not the hard part. The hard part is the system prompt — the one sentence that defines everything your tool does.
**Use Haiku for CLI tools.** Running 20x/day: Haiku ≈ $0.01/day vs Sonnet ≈ $0.20/day. Over a year: $3.65 vs $73. Use the cheapest model that produces good output.

Swap in your own system prompt and inputs:

```js
// Email reply tool
system: 'Write 2 reply options: BRIEF (2 sentences) and DETAILED (1 paragraph). Format:\nBRIEF:\n[reply]\n\nDETAILED:\n[reply]'

// Meeting summarizer
system: 'Summarize this meeting transcript. Output: KEY DECISIONS, ACTION ITEMS, OPEN QUESTIONS. Each section bulleted.'

// Code explainer
system: 'Explain this code in plain English. Assume a junior developer. 3-5 paragraphs max.'
```

Adjust `max_tokens` to output length:
- Label/category: 10 | Short answer: 100 | Summary: 300 | Document: 1000+

title: Arguments & Options
description: Let users control your tool with flags. Make it feel like a real CLI.

learningObjectives.text: Add --flags and options to a CLI tool
Handle missing inputs and API errors gracefully

contentSections.body: The difference between a script you wrote once and a tool you use every day: the tool has flags. `--verbose`, `--format json`, `--model haiku`. Flags let users control behavior without editing code.

contentSections.question: What does `process.argv.slice(2)` do? Why slice at index 2, not 0?
contentSections.answer: **Removes Node's internal values.** `process.argv[0]` = path to Node.js binary. `process.argv[1]` = path to your script file. User arguments start at index 2. `.slice(2)` removes the two system values so you only see what the user actually typed.
contentSections.hint: Log process.argv in a script and see what the first two elements are.

contentSections.body: Use a parsing library — manual string splitting breaks on spaces in values, combined flags, and quoted strings.
**Good flag design:** Only add flags users actually need to change. Model and output format are worth a flag. Indentation style is not.

title: Publish to npm
description: Make your tool globally installable. Anyone can run it with npx.

learningObjectives.text: Configure package.json for a CLI package
Publish a package to the npm registry
Handle API key setup for published tools

contentSections.title: From local script to global command

contentSections.body: Right now your tool only works on your machine at a specific path. After this lesson: `npx your-tool input.txt` works on any machine with Node installed. That's the difference between a personal script and a tool.

contentSections.question: What does `npx pr-describe` do if pr-describe isn't installed globally?
contentSections.answer: **npm downloads and runs it temporarily.** `npx` fetches the package, executes it, then discards it. No global install needed. This is why `npx create-react-app` works without a prior install — and it's the modern recommended way to distribute CLI tools.
contentSections.hint: What does npx do differently from npm install -g?

contentSections.body: `bin` + shebang = globally runnable command. Two changes, that's all.

```bash
# Setup (one-time)
npm login

# Test locally first
npm link                          # installs globally on your machine
pr-describe "$(git diff main)"    # test it!

# Publish
npm publish

# Now anyone can use it
npx pr-describe "$(git diff main)"
```

### Pre-publish checklist
- Name is unique on npmjs.com
- `.npmignore` excludes `.env`, `node_modules`, `*.test.mjs`
- README.md with install + usage + example output
- Version is meaningful (1.0.0 for first release)
**Never publish your API key.** Your `.env` must be in `.npmignore`. Users set their own `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY`.

title: Multi-Step Workflows & File I/O
description: Read files, chain API calls, write output. Build tools that work with real data.

learningObjectives.text: Chain multiple Claude API calls in sequence
Build a batch processor with rate limit handling

contentSections.body: The most powerful CLI tools don't process one line — they read a file, transform it, and write the result. Once you can read and write files, you can process entire codebases, document folders, and data exports.

contentSections.question: You want to process 50 markdown files. What breaks if you fire all 50 API calls at once?
contentSections.answer: **Rate limit errors (HTTP 429).** The Claude API has tokens-per-minute and requests-per-minute limits. 50 simultaneous requests saturate those limits immediately. Fix: batches of 5-10 with a 1-second pause between batches.
contentSections.hint: What happens when 50 requests arrive at an API in the same second?

contentSections.body: Batch API calls when processing multiple files. Process 5 concurrently, pause 1 second, repeat.
**Always log intermediate steps when --verbose.** Debugging a 4-step chain without intermediate output is painful.
